metadata.title: Economics and Computation - Fresh Exam-Style Question Set
metadata.design_notes: New questions emphasize calculations, proof steps, counterexamples, theorem selection, and cross-topic discrimination. They are intended to complement, not repeat, the uploaded 157-question bank.
recommended_use: First pass: answer priority-1 questions without notes.
Second pass: review every explanation for mistakes.
Final pass: memorize the short-answer formulas and theorem distinctions.

questions.prompt: Fix $b_{-i}$. A deterministic single-parameter allocation gives bidder $i$ the item for bids $b_i\ge 6$ and not otherwise. Under normalized DSIC payments, what does a winning bidder pay?
choices: The highest bid among all winners
questions.explanation: The threshold/critical value is 6; every winning report pays that threshold.

questions.prompt: A bidder with value $10$ faces a critical value $7$. What utility does truthful bidding produce?
questions.explanation: Truthful bidding wins and yields value minus payment: 10-7=3.

questions.prompt: Which change can destroy DSIC even when the allocation rule is monotone?
choices: Charging winners their own reports instead of their critical values.
Allowing losing bidders to pay zero.
questions.answer: Charging winners their own reports instead of their critical values.
questions.explanation: Monotonicity must be paired with the correct payment identity; first-price-style payments generally destroy truthfulness.

questions.prompt: In a second-price auction, bidder $i$ has value $8$ and the highest rival bid is $11$. Which bids maximize her utility?
questions.answer: Any bid below $11$ (including truthful bid $8$)
questions.explanation: All such bids lose and give utility 0. Truthful bidding is weakly, not necessarily strictly, dominant.

questions.prompt: Why is the normalized payment rule in Myerson\'s lemma described as essentially unique?
choices: Any two DSIC payment rules for the same monotone allocation differ only by a term independent of the bidder's own report.
There is exactly one allocation rule in every environment.
All winners must pay the second-highest bid.
Payments are uniquely determined even without normalization or individual rationality.
questions.answer: Any two DSIC payment rules for the same monotone allocation differ only by a term independent of the bidder's own report.
questions.explanation: The integration argument pins down payment differences; normalization fixes the remaining constant.

questions.prompt: For a fractional allocation $x_i(b_i)=b_i/10$ on $b_i\in[0,10]$, what is the normalized payment?
A deterministic allocation rule makes a bidder win at bid $5$, lose at bid $6$, and win again at bid $7$. What is the correct conclusion?
choices: The rule is nonmonotone and cannot be implemented by any DSIC payment rule.
The rule is DSIC with payment 6.
The rule is monotone because the bidder wins at the highest bid.
questions.answer: The rule is nonmonotone and cannot be implemented by any DSIC payment rule.
questions.explanation: Winning must be preserved under every increase in bid.

questions.prompt: In a $k$-unit Vickrey auction, why is a winner\'s payment independent of her exact winning bid?
choices: Because it is the critical losing threshold, the $(k+1)$-st highest bid.
Because the seller chooses payments after observing true values.
Because each winner pays the average of winning bids.
questions.answer: Because it is the critical losing threshold, the $(k+1)$-st highest bid.
questions.explanation: Once the bidder is above the threshold, increasing her bid does not change her payment.

questions.prompt: Which quantifier pattern correctly distinguishes DSIC from Bayes-Nash incentive compatibility?
choices: DSIC requires truthfulness for every realized $b_{-i}$; Bayesian IC requires optimality in expectation over others' types.
DSIC holds only on average; Bayesian IC holds pointwise.
Bayesian IC does not involve beliefs.
questions.answer: DSIC requires truthfulness for every realized $b_{-i}$; Bayesian IC requires optimality in expectation over others' types.
questions.explanation: Dominant strategies are prior-free and ex post; Bayesian incentives are interim and distribution-dependent.

questions.prompt: A seller cost $c=5$ and bids are $(9,7,3)$. Under the efficient second-price auction with seller cost, what are allocation and payment?
questions.answer: Bidder 1 wins and pays 7.
questions.explanation: The critical threshold is $\max\{c,b_{(2)}\}=7$.

questions.prompt: An approximation algorithm returns a feasible allocation worth at least $70\%$ of optimum, but increasing a winner\'s bid can make her lose. Which property fails?
choices: DSIC implementability in a deterministic single-parameter environment.
questions.answer: DSIC implementability in a deterministic single-parameter environment.
questions.explanation: The failure is nonmonotonicity; approximation quality does not imply truthfulness.

questions.prompt: Why can rounding in a dynamic-programming approximation create nonmonotonicity?
choices: A slightly higher bid can cross a rounding boundary and change the selected approximate solution against the bidder.
Dynamic programming cannot return feasible solutions.
Critical payments require exact arithmetic only.
questions.answer: A slightly higher bid can cross a rounding boundary and change the selected approximate solution against the bidder.
questions.explanation: Discrete surrogate objectives can change discontinuously with a report.

questions.prompt: What is a maximal-in-range allocation rule?
choices: It maximizes reported welfare exactly over a fixed, report-independent subset of feasible outcomes.
It returns an inclusion-wise maximal allocation.
It maximizes revenue over all outcomes.
It chooses the best range after seeing bids.
questions.answer: It maximizes reported welfare exactly over a fixed, report-independent subset of feasible outcomes.
questions.explanation: Exact optimization over a fixed range supports VCG-style truthfulness.

questions.prompt: Why can VCG payments be used with a maximal-in-range approximation?
choices: Within the fixed range, the rule is an exact welfare maximizer, so the standard VCG utility argument applies.
The range may depend arbitrarily on each bid.
VCG payments make any allocation truthful.
questions.answer: Within the fixed range, the rule is an exact welfare maximizer, so the standard VCG utility argument applies.
questions.explanation: The fixed range is crucial; bid-dependent ranges can break the argument.

questions.prompt: For density-greedy knapsack, bidder $i$ has public size $2$. If the critical density is $4.5$, what is her critical bid?
questions.explanation: Critical bid equals critical density times public size: $4.5\cdot2=9$.

questions.prompt: Which is the strongest correct statement about exact welfare maximization in a single-parameter NP-hard problem?
choices: It may define a truthful allocation rule but still be computationally infeasible.
NP-hardness implies the allocation is nonmonotone.
Exact welfare maximization cannot be DSIC.
questions.answer: It may define a truthful allocation rule but still be computationally infeasible.
questions.explanation: Incentive and computational requirements are distinct.

questions.prompt: An allocation algorithm uses a coin flip independent of bids to choose between two monotone deterministic rules. What truthfulness notion is naturally obtained with corresponding critical payments?
choices: Truthfulness only after observing the coin.
No truthfulness is possible with randomization.
questions.answer: Universal truthfulness.
questions.explanation: For every fixed coin outcome, agents face a deterministic DSIC mechanism.

questions.prompt: What is the main danger of choosing the approximation range after observing bids?
choices: A bidder may manipulate which range is searched, invalidating the VCG truthfulness proof.
It necessarily makes the algorithm exponential.
questions.answer: A bidder may manipulate which range is searched, invalidating the VCG truthfulness proof.
questions.explanation: Maximal-in-range requires a report-independent range.

questions.prompt: Which pair of goals best summarizes algorithmic mechanism design?
choices: Good objective value and computational efficiency, subject to incentive compatibility.
Exact revenue and budget balance only.
questions.answer: Good objective value and computational efficiency, subject to incentive compatibility.
questions.explanation: The field simultaneously handles computation, approximation, and strategic behavior.

questions.prompt: Two items are allocated together or not at all. Bidder 1 values the bundle at 10, bidder 2 at 7. Under VCG, what does bidder 1 pay?
questions.explanation: Without bidder 1, the others can obtain welfare 7; with bidder 1 winning, others get 0.

questions.prompt: In an assignment problem, agent $i$ receives a job worth 12. Other agents obtain welfare 30 in the chosen assignment and could obtain 35 without $i$. What is $i$\'s pivot payment and utility?
questions.answer: Payment 5, utility 7.
questions.explanation: The externality is $35-30=5$; utility is $12-5=7$.

questions.prompt: Which condition is essential for the standard VCG theorem?
choices: Quasi-linear utility and exact maximization of reported total value over the feasible set.
questions.answer: Quasi-linear utility and exact maximization of reported total value over the feasible set.
questions.explanation: VCG is multi-parameter but relies on transferable money and exact welfare maximization.

questions.prompt: Can a losing bidder pay a positive Clarke pivot payment in a standard single-item auction?
choices: No; a losing bidder causes no change in the allocation or welfare of the others.
Yes; every bidder pays the second-highest bid.
Yes; every loser pays her bid.
Only when her bid is zero.
questions.answer: No; a losing bidder causes no change in the allocation or welfare of the others.
questions.explanation: Removing a loser does not improve the allocation available to the remaining bidders.

questions.prompt: Why is VCG not generally budget balanced in public-project or exchange settings?
choices: Externality payments need not sum to the cost of the chosen outcome or to zero.
VCG always pays every agent her value.
Truthfulness requires a deficit in every instance.
Budget balance is identical to individual rationality.
questions.answer: Externality payments need not sum to the cost of the chosen outcome or to zero.
questions.explanation: VCG optimizes welfare and incentives, not exact revenue-cost balance.

questions.prompt: Which expression equals agent $i$\'s VCG utility under pivot payments?
questions.explanation: Utility is total welfare with the agent minus the best welfare achievable without the agent.

questions.prompt: What is the communication obstacle in a combinatorial auction with $m$ items and unrestricted valuations?
choices: Each bidder may need to describe $2^m$ bundle values.
questions.answer: Each bidder may need to describe $2^m$ bundle values.
questions.explanation: The type space is exponentially large.

questions.prompt: Why can a bidder with zero marginal contribution have zero utility under VCG?
choices: Her value in the chosen outcome is exactly offset by the externality payment.
VCG always sets utility to zero.
questions.answer: Her value in the chosen outcome is exactly offset by the externality payment.
questions.explanation: VCG utility equals marginal contribution to total welfare.

questions.prompt: Which modification generally invalidates the direct VCG truthfulness proof?
choices: Choosing an approximately welfare-maximizing outcome from a bid-dependent set.
Using deterministic tie-breaking fixed in advance.
questions.answer: Choosing an approximately welfare-maximizing outcome from a bid-dependent set.
questions.explanation: The chosen outcome no longer maximizes the objective that appears in the agent's utility over a fixed feasible set.

questions.prompt: For one bidder with $V\sim U[0,1]$, what posted price maximizes expected revenue?
questions.explanation: Revenue is $r(1-r)$, maximized at $r=1/2$.

questions.prompt: For one bidder with $V\sim U[0,1]$, what is the maximum expected posted-price revenue?
questions.explanation: At $r=1/2$, revenue is $r(1-r)=1/4$.

questions.prompt: For $V\sim U[0,2]$, what is the virtual value?
For $V\sim U[0,2]$, what is the monopoly reserve?
questions.explanation: Solve $2r-2=0$.

questions.prompt: In Myerson\'s optimal single-item auction for independent regular non-identical bidders, the item is allocated to:
choices: The bidder with the highest nonnegative virtual value.
The bidder with the highest raw value always.
A uniformly random bidder above the mean.
The bidder with the smallest hazard rate.
questions.answer: The bidder with the highest nonnegative virtual value.
questions.explanation: Negative virtual surplus is rejected; bidder-specific transformations determine priority.

questions.prompt: What problem arises when a distribution is irregular?
choices: The virtual value can decrease with value, so pointwise virtual-welfare maximization may be nonmonotone.
The density must be zero everywhere.
The seller cannot use a reserve price.
questions.answer: The virtual value can decrease with value, so pointwise virtual-welfare maximization may be nonmonotone.
questions.explanation: Ironing is used to restore monotonicity.

questions.prompt: What does ironing do in Myerson\'s theory?
choices: Replaces a nonmonotone virtual-value rule by an appropriate monotone ironed virtual value.
Converts a first-price auction into VCG pointwise.
questions.answer: Replaces a nonmonotone virtual-value rule by an appropriate monotone ironed virtual value.
questions.explanation: It pools intervals where the revenue curve is nonconcave.

questions.prompt: Why does expected revenue equal expected virtual surplus but not generally pointwise virtual surplus?
choices: The identity follows after integration by parts over the value distribution.
Payments are literally equal to virtual value on every input.
questions.answer: The identity follows after integration by parts over the value distribution.
questions.explanation: It is an expectation identity, not a per-profile accounting equality.

questions.prompt: Bulow-Klemperer requires which key assumptions?
choices: Independent and identically distributed regular bidder values.
questions.answer: Independent and identically distributed regular bidder values.
questions.explanation: The classic theorem compares Vickrey with $n+1$ i.i.d. regular bidders to the optimal auction with $n$.

questions.prompt: A reserve price is binding when:
choices: The highest bid is below the reserve, so the item is withheld.
The second-highest bid exceeds the winner's value.
questions.answer: The highest bid is below the reserve, so the item is withheld.
questions.explanation: The reserve is a minimum acceptable winning bid.

questions.prompt: In a $2\times2$ game, a player mixes with positive probability on both actions at equilibrium. What must be true?
choices: The opponent's mixture makes the player indifferent between the two actions.
The two actions have equal payoff in every cell.
The equilibrium must be socially optimal.
questions.answer: The opponent's mixture makes the player indifferent between the two actions.
questions.explanation: All supported pure strategies must give the same maximal expected payoff.

questions.prompt: In matching pennies, why is there no pure Nash equilibrium?
choices: At every pure profile, one player has a profitable unilateral deviation.
The game has no mixed strategies.
questions.answer: At every pure profile, one player has a profitable unilateral deviation.
questions.explanation: No pure action profile is stable.

questions.prompt: For a zero-sum game, what does the game value represent?
choices: The payoff the maximizing player can guarantee and the minimizing player can hold her to under optimal mixed play.
The maximum entry of the matrix.
The payoff at every pure equilibrium.
questions.answer: The payoff the maximizing player can guarantee and the minimizing player can hold her to under optimal mixed play.
questions.explanation: The minimax theorem equates these guarantees.

questions.prompt: Which strategy can safely be eliminated without removing any Nash equilibrium?
choices: A weakly dominated strategy in every elimination order.
Any strategy not currently best responding.
questions.answer: A strictly dominated strategy.
questions.explanation: Strictly dominated strategies receive zero probability in equilibrium; weak dominance is more delicate.

questions.prompt: What is an $\epsilon$-Nash equilibrium?
choices: No player can gain more than $\epsilon$ by a unilateral deviation.
The profile is within distance $\epsilon$ of the social optimum.
Every strategy has probability at least $\epsilon$.
questions.answer: No player can gain more than $\epsilon$ by a unilateral deviation.
questions.explanation: It relaxes exact best-response inequalities by an additive tolerance.

questions.prompt: Why is a mixed Nash equilibrium a product distribution?
choices: All pure profiles have equal probability.
questions.answer: Players randomize independently.
questions.explanation: Correlation is permitted in CE but not in the standard mixed-Nash model.

questions.prompt: Which statement about equilibrium selection is correct?
choices: A game can have multiple Nash equilibria with very different welfare.
Every finite game has a unique equilibrium.
All equilibria have the same payoff in general-sum games.
Nash equilibrium selects the social optimum.
questions.answer: A game can have multiple Nash equilibria with very different welfare.
questions.explanation: Existence alone does not resolve prediction or efficiency.

questions.prompt: Which is stronger for a player: having a dominant strategy or playing a best response at one profile?
choices: Playing a best response at one profile.
questions.answer: Having a dominant strategy.
questions.explanation: Dominance requires optimality against every opponents' strategy profile.

questions.prompt: In Pigou\'s network with one unit demand, latencies $c_1(x)=1$ and $c_2(x)=x^2$, what is the Wardrop equilibrium?
choices: All flow uses the constant edge.
One third uses the variable edge.
questions.answer: All flow uses the $x^2$ edge.
questions.explanation: At full flow the variable edge latency is 1, so no user can strictly improve.

questions.prompt: For the same network, if $x$ is flow on the $x^2$ edge, which objective defines the social optimum?
questions.explanation: Variable-edge contribution is flow times latency: $x\cdot x^2=x^3$.

questions.prompt: For a differentiable latency $c(x)$, what extra cost does an additional infinitesimal unit impose on existing users?
questions.explanation: The derivative of total edge cost $xc(x)$ is $c(x)+xc\'(x)$; private latency is $c(x)$, leaving externality $xc\'(x)$.

questions.prompt: With affine latency $c(x)=ax+b$, what Pigouvian toll induces marginal-cost routing?
questions.answer: $ax$

questions.prompt: What does Wardrop\'s first principle imply about two used paths for the same source-destination pair?
choices: They have equal marginal social cost without tolls.
questions.answer: They have equal minimum latency.
questions.explanation: If one used path were slower, users on it would switch.

questions.prompt: Why is the Price of Stability never larger than the Price of Anarchy?
choices: The best equilibrium cost is no larger than the worst equilibrium cost.
The optimum is always an equilibrium.
questions.answer: The best equilibrium cost is no larger than the worst equilibrium cost.
questions.explanation: Both use the same optimum denominator.

questions.prompt: A $(\lambda,\mu)$ smoothness proof has $\lambda=2$ and $\mu=1/3$. What PoA bound follows?
Why do smoothness bounds apply to coarse correlated equilibria?
choices: The proof uses only unilateral deviation inequalities that remain valid after taking expectations.
Every CCE is a pure Nash equilibrium.
questions.answer: The proof uses only unilateral deviation inequalities that remain valid after taking expectations.
questions.explanation: Linearity of expectation preserves the argument.

questions.prompt: Network over-provisioning can improve selfish-routing performance because:
choices: Operating away from capacity makes latency functions less steep, reducing congestion externalities.
It guarantees every edge has zero latency.
questions.answer: Operating away from capacity makes latency functions less steep, reducing congestion externalities.
questions.explanation: Extra capacity reduces sensitivity of latency to additional flow.

questions.prompt: Braess\'s paradox is not a contradiction to optimization theory because:
choices: The equilibrium changes after the edge is added; users do not coordinate to choose the old optimal routing.
Social optimum necessarily worsens when feasible sets expand.
questions.answer: The equilibrium changes after the edge is added; users do not coordinate to choose the old optimal routing.
questions.explanation: The old flow remains feasible, so the optimum cannot worsen, but selfish equilibrium can.

questions.prompt: In a finite exact potential game, what can be said about any sequence of strict better responses?
choices: It terminates at a pure Nash equilibrium.
It always reaches the global social optimum.
It converges to a mixed equilibrium only.
questions.answer: It terminates at a pure Nash equilibrium.
questions.explanation: Each move strictly improves a potential over a finite state space.

questions.prompt: Why need a pure Nash equilibrium not globally minimize Rosenthal\'s potential?
choices: Equilibrium corresponds to a local minimum with respect to unilateral deviations.
Every equilibrium globally minimizes social cost instead.
Rosenthal's function is not a potential.
questions.answer: Equilibrium corresponds to a local minimum with respect to unilateral deviations.
questions.explanation: Local minima can differ from the global minimum.

questions.prompt: In an atomic congestion game, one player adds an edge whose load rises from $k$ to $k+1$. By how much does Rosenthal\'s potential increase on that edge?
questions.explanation: The new final term in the inner sum is exactly the deviator's new edge cost.

questions.prompt: Which deviation class is tested by correlated equilibrium but not by coarse correlated equilibrium?
choices: A deviation conditioned on the private recommendation.
A fixed action chosen before receiving information.
A deviation to a dominant strategy only.
questions.answer: A deviation conditioned on the private recommendation.
questions.explanation: CCE permits only unconditional deviations; CE permits switching functions.

questions.prompt: Why is every mixed Nash equilibrium a correlated equilibrium?
choices: Independent equilibrium play satisfies the stronger recommendation-contingent incentive constraints.
Every product distribution is a CE.
Mixed Nash allows a mediator to force obedience.
questions.answer: Independent equilibrium play satisfies the stronger recommendation-contingent incentive constraints.
questions.explanation: Conditional on one's own sampled action, no profitable deviation exists against the opponents' independent equilibrium mixture.

questions.prompt: Which set is convex?
choices: The set of mixed Nash equilibria in every game.
The set of pure Nash equilibria.
The set of dominant-strategy profiles only.
questions.answer: The set of correlated equilibria.
questions.explanation: CE is defined by linear inequalities over a probability simplex.

questions.prompt: What computational feature distinguishes CE from mixed Nash?
choices: CE constraints are linear in the joint distribution; mixed Nash also requires nonlinear independence/product constraints.
questions.answer: CE constraints are linear in the joint distribution; mixed Nash also requires nonlinear independence/product constraints.
questions.explanation: This helps explain why CE can be found by linear programming.

questions.prompt: If a distribution is supported entirely on pure Nash equilibria, must it be a CCE?
choices: Only if it is a product distribution.
questions.answer: Yes.
questions.explanation: At each supported profile no fixed unilateral deviation improves; averaging preserves the inequality.

questions.prompt: An algorithm has regret $R_T=20\sqrt{T}$. Does it have no regret?
choices: Only if there is one action.
No, because regret must be exactly zero.
questions.answer: Yes, because $R_T/T=20/\sqrt{T}\to0$.
questions.explanation: No-regret concerns average, not cumulative, regret.

questions.prompt: Why is comparison with the best action on each individual round too strong in adversarial online learning?
choices: That dynamic comparator can exploit hindsight separately each round and is generally impossible to match.
It is weaker than fixed-action regret.
questions.answer: That dynamic comparator can exploit hindsight separately each round and is generally impossible to match.
questions.explanation: External regret uses the best single fixed action over the entire sequence.

questions.prompt: If each of $n$ actions has costs in $[0,1]$, how many rounds are sufficient for average multiplicative-weights regret $O(\epsilon)$?
questions.answer: $O(\ln n/\epsilon^2)$
questions.explanation: Set $\sqrt{\ln n/T}=O(\epsilon)$.

questions.prompt: What distribution is used when translating repeated play into an empirical CCE?
choices: The uniform distribution over the action profiles observed across rounds.
questions.answer: The uniform distribution over the action profiles observed across rounds.
questions.explanation: Average regret inequalities become CCE constraints under this empirical distribution.

questions.prompt: External regret of player $i$ against fixed action $a_i$ can be written as:
questions.answer: $\sum_t C_i(s^t)-\sum_t C_i(a_i,s_{-i}^t)$
questions.explanation: Regret is actual cumulative cost minus the comparator's cumulative cost.

questions.prompt: Why does vanishing external regret yield only CCE rather than CE?
choices: External regret tests fixed deviations, not recommendation-dependent switching rules.
CE requires zero regret on every round.
questions.answer: External regret tests fixed deviations, not recommendation-dependent switching rules.
questions.explanation: Swap/internal regret is needed for CE.

questions.prompt: In a zero-sum game, averaging the players\' mixed strategies is important because:
choices: The sequence of actual actions may cycle even while average strategies approach minimax optimality.
Every last iterate is automatically optimal.
Only pure strategies can be averaged.
questions.answer: The sequence of actual actions may cycle even while average strategies approach minimax optimality.
questions.explanation: Regret controls cumulative averages, not necessarily last-iterate convergence.

questions.prompt: Which adversary would invalidate standard regret guarantees by anticipating the learner\'s current random draw?
choices: An adversary that observes the sampled action before choosing that round's loss vector.
An adversary that knows the algorithm but not its random bits.
questions.answer: An adversary that observes the sampled action before choosing that round's loss vector.
questions.explanation: It could assign loss 1 to the chosen action and 0 elsewhere each round.

questions.prompt: When all players use no-regret algorithms in a smooth cost game, what is bounded?
choices: Long-run average social cost, up to vanishing regret terms.
The social cost of every individual round.
questions.answer: Long-run average social cost, up to vanishing regret terms.
questions.explanation: Empirical play approaches CCE, to which smoothness applies.

questions.prompt: In TTC, suppose agents 1,2,3 own houses $a,b,c$ and point respectively to $b,c,a$. What happens?
choices: The 3-cycle is executed: 1 gets $b$, 2 gets $c$, 3 gets $a$.
The mechanism chooses a random serial dictatorship.
questions.answer: The 3-cycle is executed: 1 gets $b$, 2 gets $c$, 3 gets $a$.
questions.explanation: Every directed cycle is executed, regardless of length in the standard housing model.

questions.prompt: Why is individual rationality natural in the Shapley-Scarf housing market?
choices: Each agent can block any allocation worse than her own endowment by acting alone.
questions.answer: Each agent can block any allocation worse than her own endowment by acting alone.
questions.explanation: A singleton coalition can keep its initially owned house.

questions.prompt: Which property is stronger in house allocation: Pareto efficiency or core stability?
choices: Core stability; a core allocation is Pareto efficient, but a Pareto-efficient allocation need not be in the core.
Pareto efficiency; every Pareto-efficient allocation is core.
questions.answer: Core stability; a core allocation is Pareto efficient, but a Pareto-efficient allocation need not be in the core.
questions.explanation: A Pareto improvement by all agents would itself form a blocking coalition, but smaller coalitions create additional restrictions.

questions.prompt: In applicant-proposing deferred acceptance, can a hospital\'s tentative partner become worse over time according to the hospital?
choices: No; it only replaces a held applicant with a more preferred proposer.
Yes; applicants can force a downgrade.
questions.answer: No; it only replaces a held applicant with a more preferred proposer.
questions.explanation: This monotonic improvement is central to the stability proof.

questions.prompt: Applicant $a$ prefers $h_1$ to $h_2$ and ends matched to $h_2$. What must have happened in applicant-proposing DA?
choices: $a$ proposed to $h_1$ and was rejected at some point.
questions.answer: $a$ proposed to $h_1$ and was rejected at some point.
questions.explanation: Applicants propose in descending preference order.

questions.prompt: What is the rural hospitals phenomenon in matching markets, in its basic form?
choices: The set of unmatched agents and the number of positions filled at each hospital are invariant across stable matchings under standard assumptions.
Every hospital receives its favorite applicant.
questions.answer: The set of unmatched agents and the number of positions filled at each hospital are invariant across stable matchings under standard assumptions.
questions.explanation: Stable matchings can differ in partners while preserving certain occupancy facts.

questions.prompt: Why can the receiving side manipulate deferred acceptance?
choices: The mechanism is strategyproof for proposers but not generally for receivers; a receiver may benefit by misranking or withholding options.
questions.answer: The mechanism is strategyproof for proposers but not generally for receivers; a receiver may benefit by misranking or withholding options.
questions.explanation: Stability does not imply strategyproofness for both sides.

questions.prompt: With only 2-cycles allowed in kidney exchange, which optimization problem captures maximum transplants?
choices: Maximum directed cycle cover with arbitrary cycle lengths.
questions.answer: Maximum matching.
questions.explanation: Each selected edge represents a reciprocal pairwise exchange.

questions.prompt: Why does limiting kidney-exchange cycles to length at most 3 change the computational problem?
choices: Selecting disjoint cycles becomes a constrained packing problem rather than ordinary matching.
It makes every directed graph bipartite.
questions.answer: Selecting disjoint cycles becomes a constrained packing problem rather than ordinary matching.
questions.explanation: Cycle packing with length constraints is more complex than 2-way matching.

questions.prompt: In stable matching, why is a matching with no blocking pair stable even if a coalition of several pairs could reshuffle?
choices: The standard stability concept is defined against pairwise deviations, not arbitrary coalitions.
No blocking pair implies core stability in every market model.
questions.answer: The standard stability concept is defined against pairwise deviations, not arbitrary coalitions.
questions.explanation: The deviation concept is model-specific.

questions.prompt: A monotone single-parameter allocation combined with arbitrary report-independent payments is always DSIC.
questions.answer: False
questions.explanation: Payments must satisfy the Myerson payment identity; merely being independent of the report is not enough in general.

questions.prompt: A maximal-in-range mechanism may use VCG payments because it exactly maximizes reported welfare over a fixed restricted range.
questions.answer: True
questions.explanation: The VCG proof applies to the restricted report-independent outcome range.

questions.prompt: Under Clarke pivot payments, an agent's utility equals her marginal contribution to optimal social welfare.
questions.answer: True

questions.prompt: For regular distributions, virtual values are nondecreasing, helping make virtual-welfare maximization implementable.
questions.answer: True
questions.explanation: Regularity is precisely monotonicity of the virtual valuation.

questions.prompt: Every finite zero-sum game has a pure Nash equilibrium.
questions.answer: False
questions.explanation: A mixed equilibrium always exists, but games like matching pennies have no pure equilibrium.

questions.prompt: Adding an edge can worsen the system-optimal routing cost.
questions.answer: False
questions.explanation: Expanding the feasible set cannot worsen the optimum, though it can worsen selfish equilibrium cost.

questions.prompt: Every local minimum of an exact potential is a pure Nash equilibrium.
questions.answer: True
questions.explanation: No unilateral cost-reducing deviation exists at such a state.

questions.prompt: Vanishing external regret guarantees convergence of the final action profile to a Nash equilibrium.
questions.answer: False
questions.explanation: It guarantees empirical convergence to CCE; last actions can continue cycling.

questions.prompt: Applicant-proposing deferred acceptance is strategyproof for applicants but not generally for hospitals.
questions.answer: True
questions.explanation: The proposing side has the standard dominant-strategy guarantee.

questions.prompt: TTC requires monetary transfers to compensate agents leaving a cycle.
questions.answer: False
questions.explanation: TTC is a canonical mechanism without money.

questions.prompt: A bidder wins iff $b_i\ge t$. State the normalized DSIC payment for a winner and for a loser.
questions.answer: Winner pays $t$; loser pays $0$.
questions.explanation: These are critical-value payments.

questions.prompt: Express a bidder\'s VCG utility using total optimal welfare with and without that bidder.
questions.explanation: This is the bidder's marginal contribution.

questions.prompt: For $V\sim U[0,a]$, give the virtual value and monopoly reserve.
questions.answer: $\phi(v)=2v-a$ and $r=a/2$.
questions.explanation: Use $F(v)=v/a$ and $f(v)=1/a$.

questions.prompt: Write the marginal social cost on an edge with differentiable latency $c_e(x)$.
questions.explanation: Private latency plus congestion externality.

questions.prompt: State the inclusion hierarchy among pure Nash, mixed Nash, correlated equilibrium, and coarse correlated equilibrium.
questions.answer: Pure NE $\subseteq$ mixed NE $\subseteq$ CE $\subseteq$ CCE.
questions.explanation: Each concept enlarges the allowed outcome distributions/deviations.

questions.prompt: What regret notion is sufficient for empirical convergence to correlated equilibrium?
questions.answer: Vanishing swap (internal) regret.
questions.explanation: External regret yields only CCE.

questions.prompt: Name the outcome selected by applicant-proposing deferred acceptance among all stable matchings.
questions.answer: The applicant-optimal (and hospital-pessimal) stable matching.
questions.explanation: Every applicant weakly prefers it to every other stable matching.
